retrieved_docs.title: Mandible Reconstruction -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: For osteocutaneous free tissue transfer, an incision is made in the neck, and suitable vessels for microvascular anastomosis are isolated first. Commonly, the facial artery and vein provide a suitable caliber match. The jaw and the defect are exposed through the neck, if possible, to prevent the possibility of saliva leaking onto the graft site. However, if necessary, an additional transoral incision can be made for enhanced exposure of the area to be reconstructed. The harvested osteocutaneous graft is fitted into the defect and secured to the plate with monocortical screws. In this case, two screws per segment of the graft are often sufficient, placing too many screws risks devascularization and subsequent graft failure. To restore the mandible's natural arc, wedge-shaped closing osteotomies of the bony flap can be made to permit restoration of the arched mandibular contour. However, individual segments should not be less than 1.5 to 2 cm in length, or there is an increased risk of devascularization and graft failure. Osteocutaneous grafts are harvested with their periosteum intact, as the periosteum is an important source of perfusion for the bone. Care must be taken not to disrupt the periosteum, even if making osteotomies, to maximize flap survival chances. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Transgender Surgery of the Head and Neck -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: For patients who have received hormone therapy of appropriate duration and are emotionally prepared for surgery, the choice of which procedures to undergo will be based on personal preference and a thorough discussion of goals and expectations with the surgeon. Each patient will have different needs, but frontal cranioplasty with brow ridge reduction and hairline advancement as well as mandibular angle reduction and genioplasty are very common interventions for the male to female transition. Lip lifting, rhinoplasty, and laryngeal chondroplasty are frequently selected as well, and many patients, particularly older transgender women, will also benefit from concomitant fat transfer, or cheek implants, and rhytidectomy.

retrieved_docs.title: Prosthetics in Orthopedics -- Function -- Lower Limb Prosthetics
retrieved_docs.content: Energy-storing feet include the multiaxis foot and the dynamic response foot.  The multiaxis foot adds inversion, eversion, and rotation to the traditional abilities of the single-axis foot.  The increased degrees of freedom of the multiaxis foot make it a great option for the K-2 and K-3 level ambulators who can perform even light to moderate level activity.

retrieved_docs.title: Prosthetics in Orthopedics -- Function -- Lower Limb Prosthetics
retrieved_docs.content: Level 1: Has the ability or potential to use a prosthesis for transfers or ambulation on level surfaces at fixed cadence—typical of the limited and unlimited household ambulator.

retrieved_docs.title: Transgender Surgery of the Head and Neck -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Patients transitioning from female to male are less likely to require facial surgery because of the effects of testosterone therapy but may still benefit from augmentation of the buccal fat pads or mandible. In either case, vocal surgery may also be helpful occasionally, although speech therapy to adopt more feminine cadence and intonation may be adequate on its own. Testosterone therapy will often deepen the voice sufficiently to obviate the need for voice masculinization procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: Mandible Reconstruction -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Ideally, the osseous graft ends will have good bony contact with the native mandible after inset to enhance healing. If there is poor bone-to-bone contact between the graft and mandible, then the gap can be packed with autologous or allograft bone chips to improve healing. The peroneal artery and venae comitantes are sewn to vessels in the neck to restore perfusion, and all incisions are then closed in layers. While an oral surgeon has historically placed dental implants three to six months after vascularized bone transfer to allow sufficient healing time, some patients will tolerate a longer initial operative time – so long as malignancy is not involved – that permits placement of dental implants at the time of reconstruction. This procedure is beginning to gain traction at some academic centers and is known as "jaw in a day" surgery. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Facial Implants -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Asymmetries are present in numerous congenital deformities and vary in presentation. Human skull growth occurs in multiple vectors: transverse, sagittal, and vertical. The maxilla typically grows anteriorly, inferiorly, and laterally, while the condyles grow superoposteriorly, pushing the mandible anteroinferiorly. The chin stops growing before the rest of the mandible, which continues to develop transversely and vertically. [11] Deviations from the typical growth pattern introduce mandibular rotation, creating a dolichocephalic (long, retrognathic, and vertical) or brachycephalic (short, prognathic, and horizontal) appearance. Growth usually stops at 14 to 16 years old in female individuals and 16 to 18 in male individuals. [12] Individuals with hypoplastic mandibles can correct the dental malocclusion with orthodontics if present. However, the remaining skeletal defect permits further correction with orthognathic surgery, implant augmentation, or both. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Mini-Implants -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Mini implants should be considered for retaining overdenture prosthesis as an alternative treatment when standard implant placement is not possible. Mini implants may be considered for the rehabilitation of patients who express dissatisfaction with conventional dentures and have limitations regarding the placement of standard implants. They are indicated for bridge repair and fixed replacement of the single or multiple teeth in a narrow ridge. [11] Multiple implants can be used for removable full or partial denture stabilization, and are offered at a lower cost. These can be acceptable for patients with limited economic capabilities. Mini implants in the edentulous or partially edentulous arch are indicated when the facial-lingual width of the bone is insufficient for the placement of a traditional width implant. Mini implants are also used in the anterior maxilla because of decreased palato-labial bone width and/or insufficient interdental space. In the atrophic posterior mandible, insufficient buccolingual bone width is the common indication for mini-implant placement. [12] [13] Mini implants are also widely used for orthodontic anchorage. [11] This usage can be for application of conventional anchorage to avoid forces on the reactive unit that would generate adverse side effects for asymmetrical tooth movement in all planes of space and also in some cases as an alternative to orthognathic surgery. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Fibula Free Flaps -- Technique or Treatment -- Graft Fixation
retrieved_docs.content: In mandibular reconstruction, a novel approach is the "jaw in a day" concept, involving dental implant placement into the fibula during flap transfer under the same anesthesia as bone harvesting. A provisional prosthesis is promptly fitted to the neomandible, significantly expediting oral feeding resumption for the patient. [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Transgender Surgery of the Head and Neck -- Technique or Treatment -- Frontal Cranioplasty with Brow Lift and Hairline Adjustment
retrieved_docs.content: Upon conclusion of the frontal cranioplasty, the brow lift is performed in conjunction with the closure of the incision. Because of the reduction of supraorbital bone and the release of the arcus marginalis, the brow will often elevate without much additional effort. Nevertheless, some surgeons prefer to place resorbable forehead implants to aid with fixation of the forehead flap in an elevated position during the healing process. If used, implants are placed superior to the peak of the brow, which is usually located between the medial limbus of the iris and the lateral canthus. The farther inferiorly the implants are placed, the more effective the lift will be. Still, the more visible the implants may appear beneath the skin until they dissolve, several months after placement.

The take-off point of the incision is along the inferior aspect of the defect, with the arc extending anteriorly. As mentioned above, the first arm of the spiral is sutured to the flap to initiate the spiral and recreate the alar crease or groove. The second stitch anchors the flap to the point of maximal tension along the inferior aspect of the defect. Cervicofacial Flap

retrieved_docs.title: Prosthetics in Orthopedics -- Function -- Lower Limb Prosthetics
retrieved_docs.content: An articulating knee joint is sometimes appropriate for the prosthesis. These may consist of a single axis simple hinge joint or a polycentric axis with multiple centers of rotation.  The simplest and most commonly used mechanism is the single axis hinge joint, of which the primary function is to provide articulation, allow knee flexion in swing-phase, and resist knee flexion during weight-bearing. [6] A polycentric knee joint incorporates four and six-bar mechanisms to enhance stance-phase stability and swing-phase kinematics. Although highly successful in developed countries, the cost and complexity associated with polycentric knee joints make them limited in developing countries.

retrieved_docs.title: Le Fort Osteotomy -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: The patient should then be released from maxillomandibular fixation. The mandible should be rotated in and out of the splint, if present, to confirm passive rotation in and out of centric occlusion. Centric relation should also be assessed at this stage, as any occlusal discrepancy may indicate improper condylar seating and necessitate hardware removal and replacement. Dental and skeletal midlines should also be checked to ensure the predetermined positions are achieved. The surgical site is then irrigated. The mucosa is closed in a watertight fashion utilizing a resorbable suture such as 3-0 or 4-0 chromic gut in a continuous or continuous-interlocking fashion.

retrieved_docs.title: Total Knee Replacement Techniques -- Equipment -- Constrained Hinge
retrieved_docs.content: Rotating hinge prostheses are used in complex revision arthroplasty cases with significant bone loss, ligamentous laxity, or oncologic cases. Femoral and tibial components link with an axle, and the tibial bearing can rotate around the tibial platform. This rotation allows for a lower constraint and, therefore, a decreased risk of aseptic loosening. Early implants were uniplanar without allowing rotation and had a high rate of aseptic loosening. Advantages: Very versatile and has application for many salvage cases Disadvantages: Significant bone resection needed for implant Although lower with a rotating hinge, still at risk for aseptic loosening due to increased constraint.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 989
retrieved_docs.content: THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS is classified as a subcategory of treatment; fracture or dislocation of lower extremity (other than hip or femur). Within this category, a range of specific operative interventions are enumerated, including Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (e.g., genu varus or valgus); internal fixation of femur without fracture reduction; open reduction of fracture of femur; open reduction of fracture of femur with internal fixation; osteoplasty of femur; osteoplasty, femur; combined, lengthening and shortening with femoral segment transfer; repair of malunion of femur distal to head and neck; revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure); and trochanterplasty. These procedures address various deformities, fractures, malunions, and prosthetic revisions of the thigh and leg, providing surgical options for correction, stabilization, and reconstruction. Recognizing this hierarchical relationship aids clinicians in selecting appropriate surgical interventions within the broader context of lower‑extremity trauma and orthopedic care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 209
retrieved_docs.content: The category “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” comprises a comprehensive set of operative interventions that target the femur and adjacent structures. Child procedures include epiphyseal arrest techniques—“Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); combined distal femur, proximal tibia and fibula,” “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); distal femur,” and “Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (eg, genu varus or valgus)”—which modulate growth plates to correct deformities. Fracture‑stabilizing operations encompass “Femoral shortening with blade plate,” “Internal fixation of femur” (with and without fracture reduction), “Intramedullary nailing of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur” (with or without internal fixation), and “Trochanterplasty.” Osteoplastic modifications are represented by “Osteoplasty of femur,” “Osteoplasty of femur, lengthening,” “Osteoplasty of femur, shortening,” and “Osteoplasty, femur; combined, lengthening and shortening with femoral segment transfer,” allowing alteration of femoral length and contour. Preventive and corrective measures include “Prophylactic treatment of femur,” “Repair of malunion, of femur, distal to head and neck,” and “Suture of periosteum of femur.” Prosthetic management is covered by “Replacement of prosthesis of femur with use of methyl methacrylate” and “Revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure).” Clinically, these procedures address the primary anatomical site of lower‑limb fractures, exemplified by the entity “fracture of lower limb” classified as an unspecified closed fracture of the femoral neck, underscoring the femur’s central role in diagnosis, fracture stabilization, growth modulation, length alteration, and implant revision within orthopedic care.

Procedures that modify femoral length or geometry are classified as having a procedural site rather than a direct site and comprise osteoplasty of the femur for lengthening, osteoplasty of the femur for shortening, and combined lengthening and shortening with femoral segment transfer, which are employed to correct limb‑length discrepancy and angular deformities. Indirect femoral interventions include replacement of a femoral prosthesis using methyl methacrylate, revision of total knee arthroplasty (all components), and prophylactic treatment of the femur; these are performed as part of broader reconstructive or prosthetic strategies rather than primary bone fixation. Direct‑site femoral procedures include **internal fixation of femur** (with or without fracture reduction), **intramedullary nailing of femur**, **open reduction of fracture of femur** (with or without internal fixation), **femoral shortening with blade plate**, **repair of malunion of femur distal to head and neck**, and various **osteoplasties of femur** (plain, lengthening, shortening, and combined lengthening‑shortening with femoral segment transfer). **Trochanterplasty** and **femoral‑head reconstruction (إعاضة رأس الفخذ)** are also performed directly on the femur. The procedure **internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction** (Arabic: تثبيت داخلي للكسر), a subclass of internal fixation of clavicle without fracture reduction, is applied to both “fracture or dislocation of hip and femur” and “fracture or dislocation of lower extremity (other than hip or femur)” when reduction is not performed. Both this fixation technique and **hip head replacement (إعاضة رأس الفخذ)** are members of the osteoporosis/low bone density grouping (هشاشة العظام/قلة العظام (SMQ)), highlighting the importance of bone quality in fixation and replacement surgeries. Collectively, these entities illustrate how the THIGH AND LEG surgical domain integrates fracture stabilization, deformity correction, limb‑length alteration, prosthetic revision, and reconstructive strategies centered on the femur and adjacent lower‑extremity bones, providing a unified framework for the management of complex thigh and leg pathologies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1223
retrieved_docs.content: The procedures of measuring and adjusting an unspecified external prosthesis, as well as the measurement of an arm prosthesis, are categorized within rehabilitation care that encompasses the fitting of prostheses and the adjustment of medical devices. This classification highlights that both activities are integral components of prosthetic rehabilitation, aimed at ensuring proper device fit and functionality for optimal patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 755
retrieved_docs.content: The category “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” is a subcategory of “treatment; fracture or dislocation of lower extremity (other than hip or femur)” and encompasses a range of operative interventions focused on the femur, proximal tibia, fibula, and trochanteric region. Specific procedures classified as children of this category include “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); distal femur,” which addresses growth‑plate modulation of the distal femur, and “Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (eg, genu varus or valgus),” which corrects angular deformities of the lower limb by partially arresting growth. The category also contains “Internal fixation of femur” and its variant “Internal fixation of femur without fracture reduction,” both of which provide stable fixation for femoral fractures, with the latter used when anatomical reduction is not achieved. Bone‑shaping and lengthening techniques are represented by “Osteoplasty of femur,” “Osteoplasty of femur, lengthening,” and the more complex “Osteoplasty, femur; combined, lengthening and shortening with femoral segment transfer,” which allow for correction of limb length discrepancies and deformities through controlled osteotomy and segment manipulation. Management of post‑traumatic deformity is addressed by “Repair of malunion, of femur, distal to head and neck,” which restores proper alignment of the femur distal to the proximal femoral region. Additionally, “Trochanterplasty” is listed as a child procedure, indicating surgical modification of the greater trochanter for indications such as hip abductor insufficiency or prosthetic fitting. Collectively, these procedures illustrate the comprehensive surgical toolkit available for addressing fractures, dislocations, growth‑plate disorders, angular deformities, malunions, and length discrepancies in the thigh and leg, thereby supporting optimal functional recovery and limb alignment.

Bone‑shaping and length‑altering procedures—“Femoral shortening with blade plate,” “Osteoplasty of femur,” “Osteoplasty of femur, lengthening,” “Osteoplasty of femur, shortening,” and “Osteoplasty, femur; combined, lengthening and shortening with femoral segment transfer”—modify femoral geometry to treat limb‑length discrepancies and deformities. “Prophylactic treatment of femur” and “Repair of malunion, of femur, distal to head and neck” aim to prevent pathology or correct malunited fractures, while “Suture of periosteum of femur” supports periosteal integrity for bone healing. Procedures and conditions related to osteoporosis extend beyond fracture fixation; hip‑head prosthesis (إعاضة رأس الفخذ) and multiple fractures (كسور متعددة) are also members of the osteoporosis SMQ, illustrating how severe bone‑density loss can lead to joint replacement and recurrent fractures.
